summary: German artist known for blurry 'photo-paintings' and capitalist realism. Studied/taught at the Kunstakademie Dusseldorf. Designed pixelated stained-glass windows for Cologne Cathedral using a random number generator. Created the October 18, 1977 Baader-Meinhof series, abstract Birkenau series, squeegee-technique John Cage paintings, color chart compositions, Grey Paintings, 48 Portraits, and Atlas (nearly 4000 photographs). Fictionalized in the film Never Look Away.

works.name: Photo-Paintings (Blurry/Blur Technique)
works.description: Richter's signature technique: copying photographs in paint and then blurring them. Includes portraits and other subjects. Called these works 'Abstract Painting.'

comprehensive_summary:
Gerhard Richter (born 1932) is a German artist and one of the most prominent postwar painters, widely associated with the capitalist realism movement that developed in 1960s Germany in response to pop art. He studied and taught at the Kunstakademie Dusseldorf, where he was part of a thriving contemporary art scene alongside photographers Bernd and Hilla Becher and Thomas Struth (who photographed Richter's family for his Family Portraits series). He was once a member of the Fluxus movement, and his fellow capitalist realist Sigmar Polke is known for works like Raster Drawing (Portrait of Lee Harvey Oswald) and Higher Beings Commanded.

Richter is best known for his signature 'photo-paintings,' created by copying photographs in paint and then applying his trademark blur effect, which he called 'Abstract Painting.' Before fully embracing abstractionism, he worked in a photorealist style connected to a movement that also includes Richard Estes and Chuck Close. His photo-paintings include Ema (Nude on a Staircase), a blurred portrait of his wife descending stairs that pays homage to Duchamp's Nude Descending a Staircase, No. 2, and Betty (1988), a portrait of his daughter looking away from the viewer. The black-and-white portrait series October 18, 1977 depicts members of the Red Army Faction (Baader-Meinhof Gang) using his blur technique, while Uncle Rudi is a Nazi-themed photo-painting. A blurry painting of seven sailors was sold by the Weserberg Museum to John Arnold in 2010.

Richter designed pixelated stained-glass windows for the Cologne Cathedral, consisting of 11,500 multicolored glass panes arranged randomly by a computer, installed in 2007. He also created abstract works using a homemade squeegee technique, including a series of six paintings named Cage (after John Cage) at the Tate Modern. His color chart paintings, including the 4900 Colors grid series, involve colors selected at random. The Grey Paintings reflect his declaration that gray is 'the epitome of non-statement.' The Birkenau series of four abstract works was featured in his COVID-shortened retrospective Painting After All — the last-ever exhibition at the Met Breuer. His SDI painting (blotches of yellow, black over gray) was inspired by Reagan's Star Wars initiative, and in 1994 he divided a painting into 64 squares with a carpet knife, each sold under the title Souvenir.

His Atlas project comprises nearly 4000 photographs forming a cultural history from 1962 onwards, including source images for the RAF portraits. His 48 Portraits is a curated room of images representing a canon of male Western minds. Other notable works include the Annunciation after Titian series (which grows increasingly abstract), dozens of candle and skull paintings from the 1980s (one candle painting appears on Sonic Youth's Daydream Nation cover as a memento mori), Eight Grey, and Abstraktes Bild (auctioned for a record-breaking 21 million dollars in 2010).

A fictionalized version of Richter was the subject of the film Never Look Away by Florian Henckel von Donnersmarck. In 2018, a collaboration between Richter, composer Steve Reich, and Arvo Part opened at The Shed in New York City.
